text: This is a patient in his fifties who presented to me with a posterior gist tumor close to the G junction. The gist tumor was a little bit over three centimeter close to the G junction.

I was planning initially on performing a transgastric resection with placing the port into the stomach, but then I changed my mind intraoperatively. I decided to open the gastrocolic and take down the gastrosplenic ligament and rotate the stomach and see whether I can locate the gest tumor. Posteriorly, my plan was to resect the tumor

The surgeon uses intraoperative endoscopy with TilePro to locate the posterior gist tumor near the G-junction. The tumor's proximity to the G-junction makes suturing challenging, leading to a change in plan to perform a transgastric resection by creating an anterior gastrotomy.

The surgeon uses intraoperative endoscopy to locate the site for an anterior gastrotomy. Monopolar scissors are used to create a transverse incision in the stomach, approximately three to four centimeters in length.
I honestly don't think there is one correct way of doing this procedure. I just felt that this would be the fastest and least painful way of performing the procedure.

After creating the anterior gastrotomy, the surgeon locates the GIST tumor on the posterior wall of the stomach. A blue load stapler is used for resection to prevent narrowing of the G junction, with the endoscope positioned just distal to the G junction for guidance.
The surgeon uses two loads of a stapler to resect the tumor located in the posterior wall of the stomach, ensuring the G junction is not narrowed during the procedure.
The surgeon uses TilePro to assist in avoiding narrowing the G-junction during the procedure. The GIST tumor is placed into a catch bag for removal.
The surgeon closes the anterior gastrotomy in two layers using a NINI V-Lock suture. After completion, a repeat endoscopy is performed to inspect the G junction, confirming it is not narrowed.
